808:    SUBROUTINE nnp_scale_acsf(nnp, ind, dsymdxyz, stress, active_atoms, n_active)


[...]


823:       IF (nnp%center_acsf) THEN


824:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


825:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


826:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_av(j))


827:          END DO


828:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


829:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


830:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


831:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_av(j))


832:          END DO


833: 


834:          IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


835:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


836:                nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


837:                   nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j)*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j) + nnp%scmin


838:             END DO


839:             off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


840:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


841:                nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


842:                   nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off)*nnp%ang(ind)%scale_factor(j) + nnp%scmin


843:             END DO


844:          END IF


845:       ELSE IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


846:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


847:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


848:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_min(j))*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j) &


849:                + nnp%scmin


850:          END DO


851:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


852:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


853:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


854:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_min(j))*nnp%ang(ind)%scale_factor(j) &


855:                + nnp%scmin


856:          END DO


857:       ELSE IF (nnp%scale_sigma_acsf) THEN


858:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


859:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


860:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_av(j))*nnp%rad(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j) &


861:                + nnp%scmin


862:          END DO


863:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


864:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


865:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


866:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_av(j))*nnp%ang(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j) &


867:                + nnp%scmin


868:          END DO


869:       ELSE


870:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


871:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = nnp%rad(ind)%y(j)


872:          END DO


873:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


874:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


875:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = nnp%ang(ind)%y(j)


[...]


883:       IF (PRESENT(dsymdxyz)) THEN


884:          IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


885:             IF (PRESENT(active_atoms) .AND. PRESENT(n_active)) THEN


886:                !$OMP PARALLEL DO COLLAPSE(2) DEFAULT(SHARED) PRIVATE(idx, j, k) SCHEDULE(static)


887:                DO idx = 1, n_active


888:                   DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


889:                      k = active_atoms(idx)


890:                      dsymdxyz(j, :, k) = dsymdxyz(j, :, k)*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j)


891:                   END DO


892:                END DO


893:                !$OMP END PARALLEL DO


894:                off = nnp%n_rad(ind)

903:             ELSE

920:          ELSE IF (nnp%scale_sigma_acsf) THEN

923:                DO idx = 1, n_active

928:                END DO

[...]


959:       IF (PRESENT(stress)) THEN


960:          IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


961:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


962:                stress(:, :, j) = stress(:, :, j)*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j)


963:             END DO


964:             off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


965:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


966:                stress(:, :, j + off) = stress(:, :, j + off)*nnp%ang(ind)%scale_factor(j)


967:             END DO


968:          ELSE IF (nnp%scale_sigma_acsf) THEN


969:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


970:                stress(:, :, j) = stress(:, :, j)*nnp%rad(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j)


971:             END DO


972:             off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


973:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


974:                stress(:, :, j + off) = stress(:, :, j + off)*nnp%ang(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j)


975:             END DO


976:          END IF


977:       END IF


978: 


979:    END SUBROUTINE nnp_scale_acsf

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 46.00 cycles (11.04x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 36.38 cycles (13.96x speedup).

Details


All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 508.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 6% of peak load performance is reached (8.22 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.47 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

The code analyzed by CQA in that panel excludes loops and represents 0.01% of application time for run run_0
        

          
Source file and linesnnp_acsf.F:808-979

          
Modulecp2k.psmp

        

        
The function is defined in /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:808,823-875,883-951,959-979.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 46.00 cycles (11.04x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 36.38 cycles (13.96x speedup).

Details


All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • __intel_avx_rep_memcpy: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:870
    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:874

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
